title: 🎙️ Repair Culture & Practical Tech
description: Today is about things you can fix, keep running, and pass on — repair culture, right-to-repair wins and setbacks, gear that outlives its manufacturer's support window, and the maker ethos of building to last rather than building to replace. We're interested in tools, devices, and projects where longevity, repairability, and self-sufficiency intersect, especially in rural and off-grid contexts where you can't just run to a repair shop. - Daily themed article selection for podcast generation

_podcast.theme_description: Today is about things you can fix, keep running, and pass on — repair culture, right-to-repair wins and setbacks, gear that outlives its manufacturer's support window, and the maker ethos of building to last rather than building to replace. We're interested in tools, devices, and projects where longevity, repairability, and self-sufficiency intersect, especially in rural and off-grid contexts where you can't just run to a repair shop.
_podcast.theme_scoring_prompt:
Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Repair Culture & Practical Tech' — the right to repair movement, gear that outlasts its manufacturer, DIY fixes, and tools built for longevity.

CALIBRATION — use this ladder:
- 80-100: Directly about repairability, right-to-repair legislation wins or losses, teardowns scoring repairability (iFixit-style), gear designed for longevity or user serviceability, products outliving manufacturer support windows and how owners keep them running, permacomputing, vintage or legacy hardware still in active use, open-source hardware enabling long-term repairability, Louis Rossmann-style repair advocacy, 3D-printed replacement parts extending device life, battery replacement guides, rural or off-grid repair self-sufficiency.
- 55-79: Hands-on maker and DIY content (builds, modifications, hardware hacks, workshop projects), practical consumer electronics guides with real technical depth, 3D printing hardware and filament reviews, off-grid and solar power gear reviews, home lab hardware, tools and workshop equipment, battery and power tech — even without an explicit right-to-repair angle.
- 30-54: General gadget and device reviews or news with some hands-on or technical substance; consumer electronics that touch on software longevity or ecosystem lock-in; hardware products with open firmware or modular design as a selling point.
- 10-29: Surface-level product announcements, consumer gadget speculation without technical depth, leaked device specs, general tech news without a hardware or repair angle.
- 0-9: Geopolitical conflict, natural disaster (without engineering/preparedness angle), wildlife and conservation, working lands policy, Indigenous governance, science discoveries without DIY or hardware relevance, financial market news.

Strongly prefer: articles about the right-to-repair movement (legislation, court cases, company policies), repairability scores and teardowns, gear with documented longevity, open-source or user-serviceable hardware, stories of devices kept alive past manufacturer support end-of-life, and rural/off-grid self-sufficiency through repair. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Future moon landings could wipe out clues to how life began on Earth
items.content_html:
🔧 ✏️

A new study suggests spacecraft exhaust could quickly contaminate the moon's most scientifically valuable regions, potentially masking ancient clues about how life began on Earth. Researchers say future lunar missions should consider new ways to reduce and monitor this pollution before it becomes widespread.

items.title: [Boing Boing] A company tried to make Monopoly in America and couldn't get dice
items.content_html:
🔧 ✏️

When the WS Game Company got hit with a seven-figure tariff bill on the board games it imports from China, CEO Jonathan Silva decided to find out whether he could make one in America instead. He picked a 250th-birthday edition called Monopoly: The Americana Edition and, according to NPR, hit a wall almost immediately: he couldn't find anyone in the United States to make him 10,000 dice. — Read the rest

The post A company tried to make Monopoly in America and couldn't get dice appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [Boing Boing] UK politician Ann Widdecombe beaten to death in own home
items.content_html:
🔧 ✏️

British politician and novelist Ann Widdecombe, a former minister of prisons known for her dogmatic conservative views, was found dead Thursday at her home in Devon, England. Long out of parliament and 78 years old, it was initially assumed she had died naturally, but it soon emerged she was beaten to death and that police had arrested a 26 year-old man on suspicion of her murder. — Read the rest

The post UK politician Ann Widdecombe beaten to death in own home appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [Boing Boing] Passenger nearly pulled out a Ryanair window in mid-air
items.content_html:
🔧 ✏️

A window came off a Ryanair jet mid-flight over North Macedonia on Thursday, and a passenger was partly pulled through the hole before others hauled him back by his seat belt. The flight from Thessaloniki to Memmingen turned back after debris — Greek media said it came off an engine — struck and detached one of the cabin windows, decompressing the plane and dropping the oxygen masks. — Read the rest

The post Passenger nearly pulled out a Ryanair window in mid-air appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Scientists finally solved the mystery of Earth's greatest mass extinction
items.content_html:
🔧 ✏️

Why do beaches today have seashells from clams and snails instead of brachiopods? A new study suggests the answer lies in Earth's greatest mass extinction, when warming oceans and falling oxygen levels wiped out animals that couldn't adapt. Species with body plans and metabolisms better suited to the changing conditions survived and went on to dominate the seas, offering a glimpse of how modern marine life could respond to climate change.

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Scientists found a longevity diet that helped mice eat more and lose fat
items.content_html:
🔧 ✏️

Scientists found that a modified Mediterranean-style diet with low protein and just enough methionine helped mice live healthier lives while reducing body fat and frailty. Human data also linked lower animal protein intake to lower rates of obesity and Type 2 diabetes, suggesting the approach could benefit people as well.

items.title: [MacRumors] Two Apple Stores in U.S. Are Moving Soon
items.content_html:
🔧 ✏️

Two of Apple's retail stores in the U.S. will be relocating later this month, although they will only be a short distance from their existing locations.

Apple Renaissance at Colony Park

Apple Queens Center in Elmhurst, New York is moving to a temporary location in the shopping mall on Friday, July 17 at 10 a.m. local time, presumably to allow for renovations to be completed at the existing store over the coming months.

Apple Renaissance at Colony Park in Ridgeland, Mississippi is moving to a new spot within the same shopping center on Friday, July 24 at 10 a.m. local time. Apple's new storefront will be located right in front of the Show Fountain.
Tag: Apple Store

This article, "Two Apple Stores in U.S. Are Moving Soon" first appeared on MacRumors.com

items.title: [MacRumors] Claude Cowork Expands to iPhone and the Web
items.content_html:
🔧 ✏️

Anthropic today said it is bringing Claude Cowork to mobile and the web, with beta access rolling out to Max users first. Claude Cowork is a feature that lets Claude access local files and connected tools, then autonomously complete tasks on your behalf.


Claude Cowork tasks can now be started and monitored on the web, desktop, and Claude mobile apps. Claude is able to work in the background in the cloud even when no device is online, sending an alert when it needs permission to move forward with a task.

Anthropic says the desktop is still the best place for the full Cowork experience because it gives Claude access to local files and a browser, but those who were unable to install a desktop app can now use Cowork.

Claude chat and Cowork have also been combined into a single view on the web and desktop and are no longer separate tabs.

As part of the expansion to mobile, Anthropic is extending doubled Cowork usage limits through August 5.
Tag: Anthropic

This article, "Claude Cowork Expands to iPhone and the Web" first appeared on MacRumors.com

Discuss this article in our forums
